coredata.dc:title: A rapid fluorescent aptasensor for point-of-care detection of C-reactive protein
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xuan
Liu, Xianglian
Zhang, Chuanyu
Meng, Hao
Liu, Biwu
Wei, Xueyong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report on a novel fluorescent aptasensor based on aptamers modified by both nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of C-reactive protein (CRP). FRET effect is utilized in our aptasensor by the change of aptamers conformation when binding with the target. An obvious fluorescence quench of the N-GQDs can be observed when CRP appears in the assay due to electron transfer between the donor and accepter. A detection limit of 0.2 ng/mL can be achieved by our sensor in PBS buffer which is much lower than the physiological CRP level in human serum. Also, CRP levels in different patients’ serum are tested with our assay. Since our aptasensor is rapid (detection time less than 40 min), one-step and very simple to operate, we believe it has great potential to apply for point-of-care testing (POCT).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of our FRET detection assay based on N-GQDs modified aptamers and AuNPs. The N-GQDs fluorescence emission will quench due to the decrease of distance between AuNPs and N-GQDs based on FRET effect.

(a) TEM image (scale bar of 100 nm) of AuNPs synthesized by us and a zoom-in image (scale bar of 20 nm) on bottom right. Inserted with a picture of AuNPs solution took in our lab (upper right) and the figure of AuNPs size distribution. Most AuNPs are around 11 nm in diameter. (b) Absorption spectrum of AuNPs. (c) Secondary structure of CRP aptamer simulated by M-fold website [22]. (d) Colors of AuNPs-aptamers solution with aptamers concentration from 0.5 to 3 μM. NaCl concentration was added to 600 mM and 1 M respectively. When aptamers concentration reached 3 μM, AuNPs-aptamers solution remained deep red even in 1 M NaCl, showing a good dispersibility of AuNPs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image (scale bar of 20 nm) of N-GQDs synthesized by us and a zoom-in image (scale bar of 10 nm) on bottom right. Inserted with a picture of red fluorescence emitted by N-GQDs took in lab (upper right). (b) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-aptamer-AuNPs. (c) Fluorescence emission of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-aptamer conjugate. (d) Spectrum overlap between absorbance of AuNPs and emission of N-GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) PL intensity curves when detecting different concentration of standard CRP. (b) Linear fitting for the PL intensity data from 0.2 to 300 ng/ml (F1,
R 2
= 0.99298
) and nonlinear fitting for the PL intensity data from 300 to 3000 ng/ml (F2). (c) Comparison of results when detecting 100 ng of different proteins, including CRP, BSA, cTnI and MYO. (d) Different ratios of CRP to spacer sequence including 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6 were tested with the same concentration of CRP. (e) Fluorescence response value for the detection of different patients' serum and the linear fitting line, the linear fit formula is y = 111.12992x. The lowest CRP level in serum sample measured by us based on our current method in this study, is 3.46 μg/mL.
Results for patients' serum tests from both nephelometric analyzer and our sensor.
Comparison of the sensing performance for various CRP detection methods.

row.entry: Aptamer adsorbs on AuNPs surface; monitor AuNPs aggregation with UV–Vis spectroscopy.

Easy to operate; detection signal is easy to observe.
Not accurate enough; hard to use as quantitative detection method for POCT.
Mn3(PO4)2/aptamer nanosheets for enhanced electrochemical determination; use both capture antibody and aptamer to get a sandwich structure.

Enzyme-free; with ultrasensitive detection limit.
Complicated to operate, limiting its application in labs only.
Integrated microfluidic platform equipped with immobilized highly specific aptamer probes and FET-based sensor arrays.
0.14 μg/mL in PBS buffer containing 4% BSA

Fully automated portable device; enable to detect four biomarkers including CRP.
Complicated microfluidic chip and controller leads to high price of each tests and require professionals to operate.
A SERS-based lateral flow assay, embedded gold-core silver-shell nanoparticles with built-in hot spots were conjugated with a CRP detection antibody to serve as SERS tags.
0.01 ng/mL in 10 mM PBS buffer with 0.05% Tween 20, and 25% fetal bovine serum

Detection signal is easy to observe; quite sensitive using surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) measurements.
Difficult to conduct quantitative analysis based on the colorimetric change; raman system spectrometer quite bulky and expensive.
Fluorescence polarization immunoassay using a fluorescence- labeled Fab fragment, which is the antibody binding region of the whole antibody.

Reaction complete in a simple one-step mixing, portable readout system;
Antibodies are much more expensive than aptamers
Detect N-GQDs quenching by the change of aptamers conformation when binding with targets.
one-step, very simple to operate, and able to detect human serum sample

head.ce:title: A rapid fluorescent aptasensor for point-of-care detection of C-reactive protein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Manufacturing Systems Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Manufacturing Systems Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Manufacturing Systems Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report on a novel fluorescent aptasensor based on aptamers modified by both nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of C-reactive protein (CRP). FRET effect is utilized in our aptasensor by the change of aptamers conformation when binding with the target. An obvious fluorescence quench of the N-GQDs can be observed when CRP appears in the assay due to electron transfer between the donor and accepter. A detection limit of 0.2 ng/mL can be achieved by our sensor in PBS buffer which is much lower than the physiological CRP level in human serum. Also, CRP levels in different patients’ serum are tested with our assay. Since our aptasensor is rapid (detection time less than 40 min), one-step and very simple to operate, we believe it has great potential to apply for point-of-care testing (POCT).

A novel fluorescent aptasensor is designed for the detection of C-reactive protein (CRP) based on aptamers modified by both nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). FRET effect is utilized in our aptasensor by the change of aptamers conformation when binding with the target. An obvious fluorescence quench of the N-GQDs can be observed when CRP appears in the assay due to electron transfer between the donor and accepter. A detection limit of 0.2 ng/mL can be achieved by our sensor which is much lower than the physiological CRP level in human serum.
Image 1

ce:sections.ce:para: Credit Author Statement: X. Chen and X. Liu conducted the experiment. X. Chen and C. Zhang wrote the manuscript. H. Meng provided the serum samples and conducted the CRP test with X. Chen. X. Chen, X. Liu and B. Liu analyzed the data. X. Wei supervised the whole work and was involved in the experiment design, data analysis and manuscript writing.

ce:section.ce:para: CRP is a biomarker produced by liver in human blood response to acute inflammation. Researchers have proved that CRP level in serum is closely associated with acute myocardial infarction (AMI) severity [1,2]. As reported, more than 1.5 million AMI cases occur each year in United States with an over 30% mortality [3]. The presence of chest pain is considered as one of the most important features in the diagnosis of AMI. However, not all AMI patients have this classic symptom [4]. As a classical acute phase protein, the circulating concentration of human serum CRP will increase sharply after AMI occurs, starting within 4–6 h of the onset of symptoms and reaching a peak after 50 h. It was proved by many researches that the level of CRP is not only related with the severity of AMI, but also can predict patients’ mortality over the next 6 months to a certain degree [5,6]. Thus, the rapid and accurate detection of CRP is crucial for clinical diagnosis and providing prognostic information.

Clinically, CRP is usually detected with nephelometric analyzer, which has wide measuring ranges and can detect up to 100 samples onboard at the same time. However, nephelometric analyzer is quite bulky and expensive, thus it can only be used in the professional laboratory. For the circumstances with limited medical resources, a point-of-care testing (POCT) method with as few detection steps as possible, cheap detection kits and readout machine is in urgent need. In recent years, many methods have been developed for the detection of CRP, including electrochemical method [7], surface-enhanced Raman scattering method [8], micro-cantilever method [9] and fluorescence method [10]. Among which, fluorescence detection method has its unique advantages in detection accuracy and simplicity of readout system. Besides, fluorescence method is also cheaper and easier to make into disposable chips compared to other methods, offering this method great potential for POCT.

An efficient selective element forms the foundation of a sensitive biosensor. Though antibodies have been widely used in biosensors in the past decades, researchers found that aptamers may show better performance in many aspects, including higher stability, longer shelf time, lower price and more target types. Unlike antibody production which requires antigens each time to trigger an immune response, aptamers can be synthesized without antigens once the sequence is confirmed. Moreover, aptamers are much smaller in molecular size, offering them better access to small targets like proteins [11,12].

With great help of the selective element, aptamer, how to turn biology signal detectable is the next question confronting us. For biosensors based on fluorescence method, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a useful tool used in many previous studies [13–15]. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer which occurs between a “FRET pair” consisting of a donor and an acceptor. Two basic conditions for FRET occurrence are, firstly, the distance between donor and acceptor is close enough for the energy transfer (usually around 2–20 nm); secondly, there is a spectral overlap between donor emission and acceptor absorption [16]. With this special mechanism, FRET is usually utilized as a “nano scale ruler” in biosensors. Currently, most of the CRP biosensors utilizing FRET effect are sandwich immunoassays [17,18]. The donor fluorophore and the quencher are immobilized on the capture and secondary antibody separately. When both antibodies bind with CRP, the distance between donor and acceptor will be closer, which will quench the fluorophore. Since the secondary structure of aptamers will change when it binds with a target, we take advantage of this feature to quantify the level of the target.

N-GQDs, the donor in our assay is a type of 0D material composed of single or several layers of graphene, with a size usually less than 10 nm [19]. N-GQDs synthesized by us is water-soluble, bright and completely bio-compatible which emit red fluorescence. A significant advantage to use long wavelength N-GQDs is the low autofluorescence of biological specimens in this spectrum wavelength. An electron transfer will occur when the distance between AuNPs and N-GQDs is close enough. Therefore, AuNPs are often chosen as a quencher for N-GQDs [20,21].

Herein, we proposed a one-step FRET aptasensor for the detection of an AMI biomarker CRP in human serum. N-GQDs and AuNPs are selected as the “FRET pair” in our aptasensor. Our detection limit can reach 0.2 ng/mL in PBS buffer which is far lower than the CRP level in human serum. Patients’ serum was collected from hospital and tested with our assay to compare the results with those from nephelometric analyzer. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of a FRET aptasensor based on N-GQDs and AuNPs for the detection of CRP based on secondary structure change of aptamer. Since our aptasensor is rapid in detection (40 min at room temperature), one-step, easy to operate and to detect signal, we believe it has great potential for the POCT for AMI patients to monitor their disease.

Fig. 1 shows the detection mechanism of our aptasensor. CRP aptamer was modified with amino group and thiol group on each end to immobilize with N-GQDs and AuNPs. The secondary structure of CRP is predicted by mfold website [22] (Fig. 2 (c)). Since the quite straight structure of CRP aptamer, we believe when CRP appears to the AuNPs–aptamer conjugation, the secondary structure of aptamer will fold to bind with the target, leading to a decrease of distance between N-GQDs and AuNPs. The detection mechanism is proved by our following experiments. CRP aptamer works both as a selective element for the target and a “nano ruler” for the FRET pair in our assay. Due to the FRET effect, an electron transfer will occur between the FRET pair. FRET effect turns the nano scale conformation change of aptamer before and after binding with target into detectable fluorescence change. Fluorescence of N-GQDs will be quenched by AuNPs, thus a fluorescence decrease of N-GQDs can be detected and utilized as an indicator to quantify CRP level. Schematic of our FRET detection assay is shown in Fig. 1.

In our research, CRP aptamers were synthesized from Sangon Biotech Company (Shanghai). It was modified with both amino group and thiol group on each end. The aptamer sequence is NH2-5′ CGA AGG GGA TTC GAG GGG TGA TTG CGT GCT CCA TTT GGT GTT TTT TTT TT 3′-C6-SH. The spacer sequence is 5′ TTT TTT TTT T 3′-C6-SH. Sodium chloride (NaCl), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), magnesium chloridetris (MgCl2), (2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and nuclease-free water were all purchased from Sangon Biotech Company (Shanghai, China). MES buffer (0.05 M, pH = 5.5) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.2) were bought from Beijing Leagene Biotech. Citric acid, urea, Dimethylformamide (DMF) and Tween-20 were bought from Acmec. Human serum CRP, human cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and Myoglobin (MYO) were purchased from Cloud-clone Company (Wuhan). Quartz cuvettes were bought from Aoruituo Optical Instruments Company (Yixing, China). All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were done on a JEM-2100 Electron Microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra was acquired on a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, U.S.). Fluorescence measurements were performed in a quartz cuvette with a path length of 1 cm at room temperature, through a FS5 Spectrofluorometer from Edinburgh Instruments. The absorption spectrum was obtained from Synergy™ 2 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader from Biotek.

Turkevich method was adopted by us for the synthesis of AuNPs [23]. Specifically, a 200 ml erlenmeyer flask with a magnetic stir bar were soaked in aqua regia (HCl/HNO3, 3:1) for at least 20 min, then rinsed with plenty of ultrapure water to remove impurities. An extremely clean environment is the basic of good quality AuNPs. Then, 100 ml of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution was added to the flask with continuous heating and stirring. When the solution began to reflux, 10 ml of 38.8 mM sodium citrate solution was quickly added. After the color of solution changed from pale yellow to deep red which indicating the successful synthesis of AuNPs, the solution was kept with heating and refluxing for another 20 min. Afterwards, the AuNPs solution was cooled down to room temperature under stirring. Upon synthesis, AuNPs solution was kept in dark at 4 °C for long time storage. With this method, AuNPs will be spherical and with and a uniformed size around 11 nm (see Fig. 2(a)). According to lambert–beer law, the concentration of AuNPs can be calculated by:
A =
ε c l
Where A is absorbance which can be determined in Fig. 2(b), ε is extinction coefficient, in M−1cm−1, c is the molar concentration of nanoparticles, l is the optical path length.

The extinction coefficient ε can be calculated from:
(1)
ln ε =
k l n D
+
a
Where k is a constant of 3.32111, a is a constant of 10.80505 [24], D is the core diameter of the nanoparticles. From calculation we can get to know that the concentration of our AuNPs is around 14.4 nM.
B.
Attaching aptamers to AuNPs

The purchased DNA oligos were first resuspended in nuclease-free water into 100 μM solution according to the instruction from Shanghai Sangon Biotech. Then a 1:1 v/v dilution of aptamer solution with 20 mM TCEP was prepared and left incubating at room temperature for 1 h. TCEP can effectively cleave the disulfide bond of aptamers while doesn't need any further washing step since the cleaved fragment won't influence the further adsorption of aptamers to AuNPs. Folding buffer (1 mM MgCl2, 1X PBS, pH 7.2) was used to dilute the obtained aptamers solution above into 10 μM. This 10 μM aptamer solution was heated for 5 min at 85 °C, followed by 10 min at room temperature and then 15min at 37 °C. The simple heating step will help aptamers fold into their secondary conformation.

Electrostatic repulsion between both negatively charged aptamers and AuNPs is overcome by a freezing-driven DNA adsorption strategy [25]. Compared to traditional salt-aging strategy, which takes 1–2 days to gradually increase salt concentration, freezing-driven aptamers attachment can achieve high quality AuNPs-aptamers conjugates by a simple freezing/thawing process without the help of any chemical. Moreover, freezing-driven aptamers attachment has proven to enable to align and stretch aptamers conformation while freezing [26]. In our work, a spacer sequence was hired to help aptamers attachment, which can both maintain the colloidal stability of AuNPs and create enough space for target binding. The spacer sequence solution was used after TCEP and heating treatment like CRP aptamer. Briefly, 100 μl of 10 μM CRP aptamer and 400 μl of spacer sequence (TCEP and heating treated) were added drop by drop to 1.67 mL synthesized AuNPs solution above with gentle shaking. After that, the solution was kept in −80 °C freezer for around 30 min and taken out to let it thaw. AuNPs–aptamers conjugation was centrifuged with 12000 rpm for 25 min and washed three times with washing buffer (0.01 × PBS, 0.05% Tween20) to remove the unbound aptamers.

C.
Immobilization of N-GQDs on aptamers
Red emissive N-GQDs was synthesized according to the hydrothermal method described in literature [27]. Typically, 7.5 mmol citric acid and 10 mmol urea were dissolved into 10 mL DMF and then transferred into a 50 mL stainless autoclave. The mixed solution reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. As synthesized N-GQDs were collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Lyophilization treatment was utilized to get N-GQDs powder. TEM image of N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3 (a). Synthesized N-GQDs solution can emit red fluorescence around 625 nm under 560 nm excitation. N-GQDs were immobilized on aptamers by typical EDC/NHS protocol. N-GQDs powder were first dissolved in 5 mL of MES buffer (0.05 M, pH = 5.5) as 0.1 mg/mL N-GQDs solution. 25 mg EDC was added to the solution, then 30 mg NHS was also added after 15 min sonication to activate carboxylic group on N-GQDs, followed by another 45 min sonication. Before added to the AuNPs–aptamers conjugation above, N-GQDs solution was adjusted to pH = 6.5. The condensation reaction took 4 h under stirring at room temperature.
The obtained solution was washed three times to remove free N-GQDs and finally diluted into 10 ml with 0.01 × PBS.

Experiments to figure out the proper cover rate of DNA oligos on AuNPs was done by changing DNA oligos concentration in AuNPs solution from 0.5 μM to 3 μM and adding high concentration of NaCl to the solution. Colors of the aptamers-AuNPs solution was recorded to show the aggregation level of AuNPs. From Fig. 2(d) we can see, when the concentration of DNA oligos reaches 3 μM (that is, around 208 DNA oligos on each gold nanoparticle), AuNPs can remain good dispersibility even in 1 M NaCl after a simple freezing/thawing process due to the high density of DNA oligos on their surface. Although high coverage of DNA oligos can increase the stability of AuNPs, it also compresses the space for target binding. Thus, in our assay, a short spacer sequence solution was mixed with the CRP aptamers to guarantee a full coverage of AuNPs surface. Same number of thymine (T) were designed to the end of CRP aptamer to avoid influence the affinity of aptamer.

To prove the success immobilization of N-GQDs on aptamers, Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra was tested (Fig. 3(b)). As shown in figure, characteristic vibrations of N-GQDs and N-GQDs aptamer is depicted, including the skeletal vibration from the graphene domain at 1575 cm−1, the COOH vibration peaks at 1750 cm−1 and the C–O–C vibration peak at 1064 cm−1. The peaks at 1148, 983, and 929 cm−1, are symmetric phosphate, PO, and P–O stretching vibration of aptamer respectively. Besides, as shown in Fig. 3(c), the emission peak of N-GQDs has a small blue shift after immobilizing with aptamers, which also proves the success immobilization of N-GQDs on aptamers.

An online simulation was done by M-fold webserver, which predicts the minimum free energy, ΔG, to simulate the secondary structure of the CRP aptamer [22]. The most thermodynamically stable structure was selected. 10 mM of Na+ and 25 °C were set as our simulation condition. With the possible aptamer secondary structure, we could obtain that the length between N-GQDs and AuNPs is over 15 nm before the target appear. When CRP appear, the aptamer will fold to bind the target, leading to a shorter distance between N-GQDs and AuNPs, which meets the first condition of FRET occurrence. As shown in Fig. 3(d), both the absorption spectrum of AuNPs solution and the emission fluorescence of N-GQDs were tested. A spectrum overlap can be observed from the figure, which means the second condition for FRET effect can be satisfied.

Through detecting the standard human CRP sample first, we have verified the feasibility of our aptasensor to detect CRP based on the secondary structure change of aptamer when binding with targets. Stock CRP solution was diluted with nuclease-free water into different concentration and added to the assay solution into 1 mL in a cuvette. The cuvette was left for incubation for 40 min. After that, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the sample was tested with a spectrofluorometer. The assay was excited at 560 nm and the fluorescence emission peak is around 620 nm. Results are shown in Fig. 4 (a) below. When higher concentration of CRP target appears in the assay, fluorescence of N-GQDs will decrease due to a higher efficiency of FRET. A detection limit of our aptasensor calculated according to 3σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and K is the slope of linear fitting line), is 0.2 ng/mL in PBS buffer, which is much lower than the physiological level of CRP in human serum. By the fitting curve (in Origin), a closely linear relationship can be built between PL intensity peak values and concentrations of CRP within 0.2–300 ng/mL (Fig. 4(b)). The corresponding R2 was 0.993. Therefore, we can get the conclusion that our aptasensor has a good detection performance when detecting standard CRP solution.

To verify the specificity of our proposed FRET aptasensor, several proteins including BSA, cTnI and MYO were added to the assay to compare with the fluorescence response with that of CRP (100 ng each). The fluorescence response was calculated by the difference between maximum values of the PL intensity curve. Results in Fig. 4(c) show that these interfering proteins do not generate much fluorescence change, while 100 ng/mL CRP has a strong signal response, demonstrating that our proposed aptasensor has an excellent selectivity towards.

CRP. We also adjusted the ratio between CRP aptamer and spacer sequence to get the optimal binding space for CRP binding. Different ratios of CRP to spacer sequence including 1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6 were tested with the same concentration of CRP (results shown in Fig. 4(d)). 1:4 was picked in our assay due to its high fluorescence response compared to the other groups. To further investigate the ability of our sensor to detect CRP level in human serum, 11 patients' serum samples were collected from The Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University. CRP level in these serum samples were tested with nephelometric analyzer (BN II System from Siemens) already. Considering a proper ion concentration environment is necessary for aptamers to bind with targets and the strong background fluorescence from serum, dilution of serum is a common strategy to detect biomarkers with fluorescent biosensors [28–30]. To adjust the concentration of CRP in our linear range, 2 μl of each sample was added to 998ul of our assay when detecting. The fluorescence of serum sample itself will also weaken with dilution. PL intensity was first tested as soon as the sample was added to our assay, and then was tested again after 40 min. The difference of the PL intensity was shown in Fig. 4(e). A linear relationship can also be found between concentration and PL intensity difference with a slope of 111.12992. The lowest CRP level in serum sample measured by us based on our current method in this study, is 3.46 μg/mL (has been specifically marked in the figure), which is considered as the practical detection limit in real serum samples for now. Moreover, 6 more patients' samples were collected and tested with our aptasensor to compare with the detection results from hospital based on the linear relationship obtained in Fig. 4(e). Results are listed in Fig. 5 , which are quite close to the results from nephelometric analyzer. Thus, we believe our proposed FRET aptasensor has the ability to detect CRP level in human serum accurately.

Besides, we compare the performances between our detection method and the nephelometric method in hospital. The nephelometric analyzer in the clinical laboratory we obtained our serum samples from can achieve a detection limit of 0.16 μg/mL for high-sensitivity CRP assay and a detection limit of 3.3 μg/mL for normal CRP assay. Their detection time is around 25 min. However, in the hospital blood tests, they rarely use high-sensitivity CRP assay since the detection limit is far lower than the CRP reference level in human serum. For the CRP reference level, it is usually agreed that the level of CRP in normal people's serum is lower than 10 μg/mL [31]. Especially for patients with acute myocardial infarction, serum CRP level higher than 10 μg/mL may be related to the severity of the disease [32,33]. Though our detection method cannot reach the same ultra-low LOD as the high-sensitivity CRP assay of the nephelometric analyzer in hospital, we have the comparable detection limit and accuracy with the normal CRP assay, which can completely meet the demand of clinical diagnosis. Therefore, the features of our detection method in this study underscores the potential in POCT instead of ultra-sensitive detection. In this stage, we didn't try too much on reaching lower LOD by sacrificing the simple operation and portability.

Finally, we also compare the performance of our aptasensor with several other types of CRP biosensor (listed in Table 1 .). Methods like electrochemical and field-effect transistor (FET) can obtain ultra-low detection limit. For example, the detection limit can reach 0.37 pg/mL in an electrochemical biosensor previously published [34].

However, the complexity of the detection steps and the chips or detection machine limit their possibility to be applied in POCT detection. For electrochemical biosensors, a bulky electrochemical workstation is necessary for detection and the workstation needs to connect with the electrodes on chip each test. For biosensors based on FET, the quite complex detection chips increase the price of each test. Colorimetric sensors like lateral flow strips are easy to operate and to read signal, but their detection performance cannot be used for quantitative detection. Based on the above experiments in this study, it demonstrated that FRET fluorescent aptasensor only requires one-step measurement, and is able to detect CRP level in human serum with quite cheap detection assay in each test. Furthermore, the fluorescence signal can be easily read by a portable fluorescence reader. These features pave the path to application in POCT. Based on the above experiments in this study, it demonstrated that our FRET fluorescent aptasensor only requires one-step measurement, and is able to detect CRP level in human serum with quite cheap detection assay in each test. Furthermore, the fluorescence signal can be easily read by a portable fluorescence reader. These features pave the path to application in POCT.

In this paper, we proposed a rapid and one-step FRET aptasensor for POCT detection of an AMI biomarker CRP. Aptasensor has its unique advantage on the price, simplicity to synthesize and longer shelf time at room temperature than biosensors based on antibodies. A fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon can be observed in our aptasensor when the target CRP appears to the assay due to the conformational change of the aptamer. Through this mechanism, our aptasensor can reach a detection limit of 0.2 ng/mL in PBS buffer, which is much lower than the physiological CRP level in human serum. In addition, the experiments demonstrate the linear variation of peak PL intensity with CRP concentrations and a rapid detection time of 40min. Performance of this sensor is further verified in detection of patients’ serum samples. Finally, by comparison with others, our sensor shows a good trade-off between sensitivity and simplicity. In summary, all of above results indicate that this FRET aptasensor has great potential in detecting biomarker CRP and application in POCT.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. Notes The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is financially supported by the National Key R & D Program of China (2020YFB2009002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51711530237) and Program for Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2021TD-23).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Increased monomeric CRP levels in acute myocardial infarction: a possible new and specific biomarker for diagnosis and severity assessment of disease
Atherosclerosis
